Infant Immunizations
National Infant Immunization Week (NIIW) is April 22- April 29, 2024. NIIW raises awareness about the important role vaccinations have in keeping our children, families, and community protected from diseases. Colusa County Public Health encourages you to make sure your child is up to date on vaccines by ensuring that they haven’t missed any check-ups. Well-child visits are essential.
Diseases You Almost Forgot About (Thanks to Vaccines)
Make sure your child is up to date on vaccines!
Contact your child’s healthcare provider.  For a list of healthcare providers located in Colusa County, contact Colusa County Public Health.
Vaccine Schedule for Infants and Children│ Diseases and the Vaccines that Prevent Them
Click on the name of the disease to visit the CDC web page and learn more about each disease. Vaccine abbreviations are listed to indicate which vaccine protects against each disease.
Chickenpox
(Varicella)
Diphtheria
(DTaP)
Haemophilus influenzae type b
(Hib)
Hepatitis A
(HepA)
Hepatitis B
(HepB)

(Flu)
Measles
(MMR)
Mumps
(MMR)
Pertussis
/
Whooping Cough
(DTaP)
Pneumococcal
(PCV13)
Polio
(IPV)
Rotavirus
(RV)
Rubella
(MMR)
Tetanus
(DTaP)
CDC Recommended Vaccines by Age
Vaccines at Birth
Vaccines from 1 to 2 months
Vaccines at 4 months
Vaccines at 6 months
Vaccines from 7 to 11 months
Vaccines from 12 to 23 Months
Vaccines from 2 to 3 Years
When the time comes for your child to get his or her vaccines, here are some useful tips about what to expect and how you can prepare:
Vaccine Visit
Why Vaccinate
Parents work hard to keep their babies healthy and safe. But, even healthy babies need vaccines to protect them from serious diseases. By vaccinating them according to CDC's recommended schedule, parents can protect their babies from 14 serious diseases before they turn 2 years old.
